• To demonstrate the efficacy of [AMENDMENT_A101] vaccine to prevent primary CMV infection in CMV-seronegative female participants.

• Primary CMV infection, defined as seroconversion from a negative to a positive result for serum [INVESTIGATIONAL_PRODUCT_A] as measured by a platform-based automated immunoassay based on at least 1 of the 4 recombinant CMV antigens not encoded by [AMENDMENT_A101] (pp150, pp28, pp52, pp38) assessed starting 28 days after the third injection.
• To evaluate the safety and reactogenicity of [AMENDMENT_A101] vaccine when administered on a 3-dose injection schedule in all participants.

• Solicited ARs through 7 days after each injection, unsolicited AEs through 28 days after each injection, MAAEs from Day 1 through 6 months after the last injection, AESIs from Day 1 through EoS, and SAEs from time of consent through EoS.
• To evaluate the immunogenicity to mRNA- 1647 when administered on a 3-dose injection schedule in all participants.
• Antigen-specific nAb and binding antibody GMTs or GMCs on Day 1, Month 3, Month 7, and Month 12.
• To evaluate persistence of immunogenicity to [AMENDMENT_A101] through 24 months after the third injection in all participants.
• Antigen-specific nAb and binding antibody GMTs or GMCs on Month 18, Month 24, and Month 30.
• To demonstrate the efficacy of [AMENDMENT_A101] to prevent systemic viral dissemination in participants who meet the endpoint of CMV seroconversion.

• Systemic viral dissemination defined as seroconversion as described in the primary efficacy endpoint AND meeting one of the following PCR criteria during the post-seroconversion period: a) Positive CMV PCR results in both blood and urine (concurrently or at different timepoints) OR b) Positive CMV PCR results in urine at ≥2 timepoints
• To evaluate the efficacy of [AMENDMENT_A101] to reduce the frequency of urinary CMV shedding in CMV-seronegative participants meeting the endpoint of seroconversion due to primary CMV infection.
• CMV PCR results in urine at each post-seroconversion timepoint and during the entire post-seroconversion period.

• To evaluate the effect of [AMENDMENT_A101] on CMV infection kinetics in CMV-seronegative participants meeting the endpoint of seroconversion due to primary CMV infection as measured by CMV PCR of blood, urine, and saliva. • To evaluate the effect of [AMENDMENT_A101] on the frequency and level of urinary CMV shedding in CMV-seropositive participants. • In CMV-seronegative participants receiving [AMENDMENT_A101], to evaluate levels of antigen-specific nAb and binding antibodies in participants meeting the endpoint of seroconversion due to primary CMV infection and participants not meeting the endpoint of seroconversion due to primary CMV infection. • To evaluate the effect of [AMENDMENT_A101] on incidence of symptomatic primary CMV infection in CMV-seronegative participants. • To evaluate immune markers after receipt of [AMENDMENT_A101] vaccine as correlates of risk for primary CMV infection in CMV-seronegative participants. • To describe the effect of [AMENDMENT_A101] vaccine to prevent primary CMV infection in CMV-seronegative participants who received 2 doses of a 3-dose regimen. • To evaluate the impact of symptomatic primary CMV infection on participant-reported health state and health-related productivity in CMV-seronegative participants.

• Vaccine efficacy of [AMENDMENT_A101] to prevent primary CMV infection starting 28 days after the third injection. A primary CMV infection case is defined as seroconversion from a negative to a positive result for serum [INVESTIGATIONAL_PRODUCT_A] as measured by a platform-based automated immunoassay based on at least 1 of 4 recombinant CMV antigens not encoded by [AMENDMENT_A101] (pp150, pp28, pp52, pp38) and confirmed by a repeat [INVESTIGATIONAL_PRODUCT_A] test performed ≥28 days from the date of seroconversion.

• Primary analysis: VE will be estimated with 1 – HR ([AMENDMENT_A101] vs. placebo) using a stratified Cox proportional hazard model to assess the magnitude of the treatment group difference (ie, HR) between mRNA‑1647 versus placebo. Cases will be counted starting 1 month (28 days) after the third injection. • Supportive analysis: Analysis using the same model based on the mITT Set. • Sensitivity analysis using the same model based on the PP Set, with cases counted starting after the third injection, from 1 month (28 days) after the second injection, after the second injection, after the first injection, and after randomization, separately. • Supportive analysis: VE to be estimated based on the incidence rate of primary CMV infection in each treatment group, defined as (1 – relative risk), where relative risk is the incidence rate of the primary CMV infection in participants in the [AMENDMENT_A101] group relative to that of the placebo group ([AMENDMENT_A101]/placebo). The 2-sided 95% CI of VE will be provided using the exact method conditional upon the total number of cases based on Poisson distribution.

2. To assess for the presence of clinical diagnosis of cCMV infection in infants meeting any of the following criteria: • have abnormalities reported on the [AMENDMENT_A101]-P301 main study Pregnancy Report Form; • meet the exploratory endpoint under Objective 1 for positive CMV PCR results; • are born to CMV-seronegative participants who meet the endpoint of seroconversion due to 1°CMV infection prior to or during the pregnancy.

Presence of clinical diagnosis of cCMV infection will be determined based on the Investigator’s review of infant and/or participant medical records documenting one of the following results: • any positive CMV PCR results of neonatal urine or saliva + urine collected within the first 3 weeks of life or • in CMV-seronegative participants who meet the endpoint of seroconversion due to 1°CMV infection prior to or during the pregnancy, who had a positive CMV PCR result of amniotic fluid after 20 weeks and more than 8 weeks after presumed onset of maternal primary infection.
3. In infants with a clinical diagnosis of cCMV infection as determined by Objective 2, to assess for presence of cCMV disease and to assess for the level of cCMV disease severity.

Presence of signs/symptoms of cCMV disease will be determined based on the Investigator’s review of infant medical records for documentation of any of the signs/symptoms listed in Section 11.6.7.4.1 (Luck 2017). Level of cCMV disease severity will be determined by the signs/symptoms documented from the Investigator’s review of infant medical records based on the severity criteria listed in Section 11.6.7.4.1 (Rawlinson et al 2017).

Section 2.3.2 Risks from Study Participation and Their Mitigation

Updated: There have been very rare (<1 in 10,000 recipients) reports of myocarditis and pericarditis occurring after vaccination with coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) mRNA vaccines. The majority of the cases have been reported in young males, within 7 to 14 days shortly after the second dose of the vaccine. These are typically mild cases and individuals tend to recover within a short time following standard treatment (including rest, NSAIDs, and/or colchicine). and rest Healthcare professionals and study participants should be alert to the signs and symptoms of myocarditis and pericarditis (Gargano et al 2021). It is not known whether the risk of myocarditis or pericarditis is increased following additional doses of other non-COVID mRNA vaccines (Gargano et al 2021). Investigators and participants should be alert to the signs and symptoms of myocarditis and pericarditis.
To align with [SPONSOR_A] position on clinical management and CEAC review of myocarditis, pericarditis, and/or myopericarditis.

Added: The Investigator’s medical judgment must be applied when assessing participants reporting symptoms for myocarditis, pericarditis, and/or myopericarditis contained within the CDC case definition. Diagnostic evaluation (eg, ECG, echocardiogram) and laboratory testing (eg, troponin) included in the CDC definition (Section 11.7) should promptly be obtained if considered clinically indicated in any participant with concerning signs/symptoms. significant symptoms without other identifiable causes. Additional testing and evaluation may be indicated. The Investigator will submit any updated myocarditis, pericarditis, and/or myopericarditis case data to the Sponsor within 24 hours of it being available. Cases of myocarditis, pericarditis, and/or myopericarditis will be followed until resolution of symptoms and abnormal test findings. Participants with events of myocarditis, pericarditis, and/or myopericarditis will be discontinued from further study injection but should continue to be followed in the study for safety as per the protocol. An independent CEAC will review all suspected cases of myocarditis, pericarditis, and myopericarditis, which are reported in ongoing interventional clinical studies per the CEAC charter, to determine if they meet CDC criteria for “probable” or “confirmed” events (Section 11.7). In the case of any SAE or AESI, the participant must be followed up until clinical recovery is complete or until the outcome has been stabilized.

cells.text: Section 9.2. Statistical Hypotheses; Section 9.3.1. Power and Sample Size for the Cytomegalovirus- seronegative Cohort; Section 9.5. Statistical Analyses; Section 9.6. Planned Analyses

The second inferential analysis of efficacy was changed from the End-of-Study Analysis to a Final Analysis that will be carried out when at least a total of 112 cases of the primary efficacy endpoint have been observed across the 2 groups in the PP Set for Efficacy, without separately requiring reaching the end of the main study. The End-of-Study Analysis was changed from an inferential analysis of efficacy to a supportive analysis.

The enrollment of this study was slower than initially anticipated. In the event that VE is not demonstrated at the Primary Analysis, changing the timing of the second inferential analysis of efficacy to be tr[INVESTIGATIONAL_PRODUCT_A]ered by the accrual of a prespecified number of primary efficacy endpoint cases alone will enable the Sponsor to potentially have an earlier second inferential analysis of efficacy, without compromising statistical rigor. The overall Type I error rate for this study remains strictly controlled at 2.5% (one-sided) with 0.5% allocated to the Primary Analysis and 2.0% allocated to the Final Analysis.
